Refactor_Advice: Analyze the algorithm's time complexity. Replace inefficient algorithms (e.g., nested loops) with optimized data structures or algorithms.
Review conditional and mathematical expressions to ensure the correct operator is used (e.g., using '==' instead of '=').
Identify and remove unreachable code segments, unused functions, or obsolete conditional branches to reduce maintenance overhead.
Refactor the logical condition. Remove the dead conditional block or correct the logic so it can evaluate dynamically.
Refactor the logical condition. Remove the redundant check and execute the code block directly, or fix the logic.
Use explicit parentheses in complex expressions to force the intended order of operations and clarify logic.
Extract duplicate or redundant code patterns shared across multiple locations into a single, reusable function or method.

Description: Excessive Use of Hard-Coded Literals in Initialization
Refactor_Advice: Replace hard-coded literal strings and 'magic numbers' with named constants, enums, or configuration parameters.

Description: Loop Condition Value Update within the Loop
Refactor_Advice: Ensure loop control variables are updated exclusively in the loop signature to prevent unpredictable control flow logic.
Reduce branching by extracting complex nested if/else logic into smaller helper functions or using polymorphism.
Guarantee resource closure by utilizing try-with-resources statements or explicit 'finally' blocks.

Description: Missing Release of Resource after Effective Lifetime
Refactor_Advice: Release network connections, threads, or memory immediately after their functional use has completed.

Description: Missing Release of File Descriptor or Handle after Effective Lifetime
Refactor_Advice: Always explicitly close file descriptors/handles in error-handling 'finally' blocks to prevent descriptor exhaustion.

Description: Excessive Platform Resource Consumption within a Loop
Refactor_Advice: Extract resource-heavy operations (e.g., database connections, file I/O) out of the loop body.
Explicitly verify the return values of all method calls, particularly I/O and system APIs, and implement appropriate error-handling logic.

Description: Detection of Error Condition Without Action
Refactor_Advice: Populate empty catch blocks or error branches with meaningful handling logic, such as structured logging or state recovery.
Ensure all temporary files, caches, and supporting resources are systematically deleted or scrubbed after their designated use.

Description: Use of Wrong Operator in String Comparison
Refactor_Advice: Utilize dedicated string comparison methods rather than identity operators when evaluating string equivalence.
Ensure complex resources are fully initialized and valid before their references are exposed to the rest of the application.

Description: Operation on a Resource after Expiration or Release
Refactor_Advice: Invalidate resource references immediately upon release and verify state flags before attempting subsequent operations.
Check bounds before downcasting primitive types (e.g., 64-bit to 32-bit) to prevent high-order bit data loss.
Audit arithmetic logic for critical decision-making or resource management code, adding comprehensive unit tests for mathematical correctness.
Add conditional guards to explicitly verify that the denominator is strictly non-zero before executing any division.

Description: Improper Check or Handling of Exceptional Conditions
Refactor_Advice: Anticipate edge cases and implement comprehensive try-catch mechanisms to handle conditions that disrupt normal flow.
Ensure all thrown exceptions are caught and handled by an appropriate upstream catch block or global exception handler.
Do not ignore error conditions or swallow exceptions silently; log them properly and handle the state transition.
Ensure functions encountering errors explicitly return informative status codes or throw structured exceptions to the caller.

Description: Loop with Unreachable Exit Condition ('Infinite Loop')
Refactor_Advice: Review loop control variables to ensure they are properly updated within the loop body to reach the termination condition.
Ensure resources and connections are completely initialized and verified before exposing them to business logic execution.

Description: Floating Point Comparison with Incorrect Operator
Refactor_Advice: Compare floating-point values using an acceptable tolerance threshold (epsilon) rather than direct strict equality operators (==).

Description: Improper Limitation of a Pathname to a Restricted Directory ('Path Traversal')
Refactor_Advice: Validate all filesystem path inputs against a strict allowlist and resolve paths to their canonical absolute format before access.
Neutralize relative path sequences (like '../') by strictly validating inputs against allowed directory scopes.
Reject any input containing absolute path definitions and construct file paths programmatically from a safe base directory.

Description: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in a Command ('Command Injection')
Refactor_Advice: Avoid shelling out to the OS. Use safe, parameterized API bindings that bypass the shell interpreter entirely.

Description: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an OS Command ('OS Command Injection')
Refactor_Advice: Do not pass user input directly into OS command execution functions; strictly sanitize inputs and use execution APIs that separate command from arguments.
Strictly delimit and sanitize all arguments passed to downstream components or processes to prevent flag/argument injection.

Description: Improper Control of Resource Identifiers (‘Resource injection’)
Refactor_Advice: Implement strict validation and access control checks before allowing external input to dictate resource identifiers (e.g., port numbers, file IDs).
Validate array indices derived from untrusted input against strict upper and lower bounds before using them for data access.

Description: Use of Externally Controlled Format String
Refactor_Advice: Never pass unvalidated user input directly as the format string parameter to formatting functions (e.g., printf); use static strings.
Replace deprecated or obsolete functions with their modernized, secure equivalents provided by updated standard libraries.
Avoid deserializing complex objects from untrusted sources; utilize safe data formats like JSON or enforce strict class allowlists.
Sanitize and strictly bound inputs used for loop conditions to prevent attackers from causing excessive CPU consumption.
Enforce strict upper limits and validation checks on size parameters derived from external inputs before dynamic memory allocation.
Extract all hard-coded secrets from the source code and inject them dynamically via secure environment variables or a vault.
Do not commit passwords into version control. Abstract authentication secrets into external configuration files or secret managers.
Manage cryptographic keys via secure key management systems (KMS) or HSMs rather than embedding them directly in source code.
